modules.title: Module 1: Python Basics & Variables
modules.description: Learn print statements, comments, variables, and basic data types.

steps.title: 1. Making Python Speak
steps.concept: In Python, we tell the computer to display text using the print() function. Think of it like a megaphone: whatever text you put inside quotes " " and parentheses ( ) will be displayed on the screen.
steps.instructions: Click Load Demo Code to load this example into your editor, then click Run Code to run it!

steps.title: 2. Writing Notes (Comments)
steps.concept: Comments are notes for humans. Any line that starts with a hash symbol # is completely ignored by Python. They are great for explaining what your code does.
steps.demo_code:
# This is a comment. Python ignores this!
print("Python is running this line!")
steps.instructions: Click Load Demo Code and click Run Code. You will see that only the text inside the print function is printed!

steps.title: 3. Variables (Labeled Drawers)
steps.concept:
A variable is like a labeled storage drawer. You store data in it and name the drawer. To store something, we use the equals = sign (assignment operator).

label_name = value_to_store
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change "Panda" to your favorite animal's name, then click Run Code.

steps.title: 4. Numbers and Decimals (Integers & Floats)
steps.concept:
Python categorizes numbers into two main types:

  • Integers (int): Whole numbers (positive, negative, or zero), e.g., 10.
  • Floats (float): Decimal numbers, e.g., 1.99.

You can perform math operations on them directly.
steps.demo_code:
candies = 10
price = 1.99
total_cost = candies * price
print(total_cost)
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change candies to 5 and click Run Code to calculate the new total cost!

steps.title: 5. Text Formatting (F-Strings)
steps.concept: An F-string lets you inject variables directly inside text. Put an f right before the opening quotation mark, and write variables inside curly braces { }.
steps.demo_code:
name = "Bob"
score = 95
print(f"{name} scored {score}% on the quiz!")
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change the name from "Bob" to your own name and run the code!

labs.title: Exercise 1: Labeled Boxes
labs.instructions: Create a variable named student_name and set it to "Alex". Then, create a variable named hours_studied and set it to the number 4.
labs.initial_code:
# Write your code below:
student_name = ""
hours_studied = 0
labs.example_code:
# Example of creating variables:
player_name = "Mario"
score = 150
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
student_name = "Alex"
hours_studied = 4
labs.instructions_variant: Create a variable named employee_name and set it to "Sarah". Then, create a variable named years_service and set it to the number 5.
labs.initial_code_variant:
# Write your code below:
employee_name = ""
years_service = 0
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of creating variables:
player_name = "Luigi"
score = 250
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
employee_name = "Sarah"
years_service = 5

labs.title: Exercise 2: Basic Calculations
labs.instructions: Create a variable base_salary and set it to 1200. Create a variable bonus and set it to 350. Finally, create a variable total_pay and set it to the sum of the two variables.
labs.example_code:
# Example of performing basic math on variables:
item_price = 10
tax = 2
total_price = item_price + tax
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
base_salary = 1200
bonus = 350
total_pay = base_salary + bonus
labs.instructions_variant: Create a variable rent and set it to 800. Create a variable utilities and set it to 150. Finally, create a variable total_expenses and set it to the sum of the two variables.
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of adding variables:
item_price = 100
shipping = 15
total_cost = item_price + shipping
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
rent = 800
utilities = 150
total_expenses = rent + utilities

labs.title: Exercise 3: String Formatting (F-Strings)
labs.instructions: Create a variable city with value "Paris" and country with value "France". Then create travel_message using an F-string that evaluates to: "I want to visit Paris, France."
labs.example_code:
# Example of using F-strings to format text:
fruit = "apple"
color = "red"
message = f"The {fruit} is {color}."
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
city = "Paris"
country = "France"
travel_message = f"I want to visit {city}, {country}."
labs.instructions_variant: Create a variable movie with value "Toy Story" and genre with value "Animation". Then create movie_message using an F-string that evaluates to: "The movie Toy Story is in the Animation genre."
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of using F-strings:
pet = "dog"
name = "Buddy"
status_msg = f"My pet {pet} is named {name}."
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
movie = "Toy Story"
genre = "Animation"
movie_message = f"The movie {movie} is in the {genre} genre."

labs.title: Exercise 4: Slices (Modulo & Division)
labs.instructions: Create a variable total_slices set to 15. Create slices_per_person and set it to the number of whole slices each of 4 people get (using floor division //). Create leftover_slices and set it to the remainder (using modulo %).
labs.example_code:
# Example of whole division (floor division) and remainder (modulo):
total_cookies = 10
cookies_per_child = total_cookies // 3
remaining_cookies = total_cookies % 3
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
total_slices = 15
slices_per_person = total_slices // 4
leftover_slices = total_slices % 4
labs.instructions_variant: Create a variable total_candies set to 22. Create candies_per_child and set it to the number of whole candies each of 5 children get (using floor division //). Create leftover_candies and set it to the remainder (using modulo %).
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of division and modulo:
total_pencils = 17
pencils_per_box = total_pencils // 6
remaining_pencils = total_pencils % 6
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
total_candies = 22
candies_per_child = total_candies // 5
leftover_candies = total_candies % 5

modules.title: Module 2: Control Flow & Logic
modules.description: Learn comparisons, conditional statements, logical operators, and loops.

steps.title: 1. Comparing Values
steps.concept: Comparison operators ask Python a question. The result is always a Boolean: True (yes) or False (no). We use: == (equal to), != (not equal), > (greater than), and < (less than).
steps.demo_code:
cookies = 5
print("Is cookies > 3?", cookies > 3)
print("Is cookies equal to 10?", cookies == 10)
steps.instructions: Load the demo code and run it to see how Python evaluates comparison expressions.

steps.title: 2. If, Elif, and Else Decisions
steps.concept: Use if, elif (else if), and else to make decisions in code. Python runs different blocks based on conditions. Don't forget to **indent** (4 spaces) the lines inside the blocks!
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change the temperature variable to 5 and run it to see Python switch to the coat path.

steps.title: 3. Repeating Tasks (For Loops)
steps.concept: A for loop repeats a block of code a set number of times. The range(start, end) function generates count numbers from start up to (but not including) end.
steps.demo_code:
for count in range(1, 4):
 print(f"This is loop turn {count}")
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change the range to count from 1 to 6 and run the code!

steps.title: 4. Loops with Conditions (While Loops)
steps.concept: A while loop runs continuously as long as a condition is True. Ensure you change the variable inside the loop so the condition eventually becomes False and stops, otherwise it will run forever!
steps.demo_code:
battery = 3
while battery > 0:
 print(f"Phone active. Battery: {battery}%")
 battery = battery - 1
print("Battery dead!")
steps.instructions: Load the demo and run it. Notice how the battery level decreases by 1 in each turn until it stops.

labs.title: Exercise 1: Number Sign Classifier
labs.instructions: Create a variable number set to -7.5. Create a variable classification and use an if-elif-else statement to set it to "positive", "negative", or "zero" depending on the value of number.
labs.initial_code:
# Write your code below:
number = -7.5
classification = ""
labs.instructions_variant: Create a variable score set to 85. Create a variable result_status and use an if-elif-else statement to set it to "pass" (if score >= 60), "fail" (if score < 60), or "error" (if score is less than 0 or greater than 100).
labs.initial_code_variant:
# Write your code below:
score = 85
result_status = ""

labs.title: Exercise 2: Multiply by 10 (Loops)
labs.instructions: Create an empty list called results. Use a for loop to count from 1 to 10. Inside, multiply the current number by 10 and add (.append()) the result to results.
labs.example_code:
# Example of creating a list and adding items using a loop:
values = []
for x in range(1, 5):
 values.append(x * 2)
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
results = []
for i in range(1, 11):
 results.append(i * 10)
labs.instructions_variant: Create an empty list called squares. Use a for loop to count from 1 to 5. Inside, square the current number (multiply it by itself, or use ** 2) and add (.append()) the result to squares.
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of building a list with loops:
doubles = []
for x in range(1, 6):
 doubles.append(x * 2)
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
squares = []
for i in range(1, 6):
 squares.append(i * i)

labs.title: Exercise 3: Battery Charge (While Loops)
labs.instructions: Create variables charge = 0 and ticks = 0. Write a while loop that runs while charge < 100. Inside the loop, increase charge by 15 and ticks by 1.
labs.initial_code:
# Write your code below:
charge = 0
ticks = 0
labs.example_code:
# Example of running a loop while a condition is true:
water_level = 0
steps = 0
while water_level < 50:
 water_level = water_level + 10
 steps = steps + 1
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
charge = 0
ticks = 0
while charge < 100:
 charge = charge + 15
 ticks = ticks + 1
labs.instructions_variant: Create variables distance = 0 and steps = 0. Write a while loop that runs while distance < 80. Inside the loop, increase distance by 12 and steps by 1.
labs.initial_code_variant:
# Write your code below:
distance = 0
steps = 0
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of while loop count:
level = 0
iterations = 0
while level < 50:
 level = level + 8
 iterations = iterations + 1
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
distance = 0
steps = 0
while distance < 80:
 distance = distance + 12
 steps = steps + 1

modules.title: Module 3: Data Structures
modules.description: Learn Lists, Dictionaries, Tuples, and Sets.

steps.title: 1. Lists (Ordered Collections)
steps.concept: A list is an ordered group of items written inside square brackets [ ]. You retrieve items using an index starting at 0. Use negative indexes (like -1) to count from the end.
steps.demo_code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
print("First fruit:", fruits[0])
print("Last fruit:", fruits[-1])
steps.instructions: Load the demo and run it. Try changing index 0 to index 1 to get the banana!

steps.title: 2. Modifying Lists
steps.concept: Lists are mutable (changeable). Use .append(item) to add an item to the end, and .remove(item) to remove it by value.
steps.demo_code:
playlist = ["Song A", "Song B"]
playlist.append("Song C")
playlist.remove("Song A")
print(playlist)
steps.instructions: Load the demo and run it to see the updated playlist.

steps.title: 3. Dictionaries (Key-Value Lookups)
steps.concept: A dictionary stores key-value pairs inside curly braces { }. Use a unique key (like a name) to find its value (like a phone number or price).
steps.demo_code:
prices = {"apple": 0.99, "banana": 0.59}
prices["orange"] = 0.79 # Adds orange
print("An apple costs:", prices["apple"]) 
print(prices)
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change the apple price to 1.20 and run the code.

labs.title: Exercise 1: Shopping List Manager
labs.instructions: Create a list shopping_list containing "apples", "milk", and "bread". Add "eggs" to the end. Remove "milk". Find the length and store it in list_length.
labs.example_code:
# Example of list operations:
colors = ["red", "blue"]
colors.append("green") # Adds to the end
colors.remove("red") # Removes by value
num_colors = len(colors) # Finds length
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
shopping_list = ["apples", "milk", "bread"]
shopping_list.append("eggs")
shopping_list.remove("milk")
list_length = len(shopping_list)
labs.instructions_variant: Create a list todo_list containing "clean", "study", and "exercise". Add "read" to the end. Remove "study". Find the length and store it in todo_length.
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of list append and remove:
tags = ["a", "b"]
tags.append("c")
tags.remove("b")
tags_count = len(tags)
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
todo_list = ["clean", "study", "exercise"]
todo_list.append("read")
todo_list.remove("study")
todo_length = len(todo_list)

labs.title: Exercise 2: Inventory Lookup
labs.instructions: Create a dictionary inventory with keys: "apples" set to 12, "oranges" set to 5, and "bananas" set to 15. Retrieve the bananas quantity and store it in bananas_qty.
labs.instructions_variant: Create a dictionary prices with keys: "milk" set to 3.50, "bread" set to 2.20, and "cheese" set to 4.80. Retrieve the cheese price and store it in cheese_cost.

labs.title: Exercise 3: Common Colors (Sets)
labs.instructions: Alice likes colors ["blue", "green", "red", "green"]. Bob likes ["green", "yellow", "blue"]. Convert both lists to sets (alice_set and bob_set), and find their common colors as a set in shared_colors using the intersection .intersection() method.
labs.example_code:
# Example of converting lists to sets and finding common items:
team_a = set(["apple", "banana"])
team_b = set(["banana", "cherry"])
common_items = team_a.intersection(team_b)
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
alice_set = set(["blue", "green", "red", "green"])
bob_set = set(["green", "yellow", "blue"])
shared_colors = alice_set.intersection(bob_set)
labs.instructions_variant: Group A has members ["cat", "dog", "bird", "dog"]. Group B has members ["dog", "fish", "cat"]. Convert both lists to sets (set_a and set_b), and find their common members in shared_members using the intersection .intersection() method.
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of finding set intersection:
s1 = set([1, 2, 3])
s2 = set([2, 3, 4])
common = s1.intersection(s2)
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
set_a = set(["cat", "dog", "bird", "dog"])
set_b = set(["dog", "fish", "cat"])
shared_members = set_a.intersection(set_b)

modules.title: Module 4: Functions & Reusable Code
modules.description: Learn parameters, return statements, scope, and libraries.

steps.title: 1. Writing Custom Functions
steps.concept: A function is a reusable block of code. Define it with def function_name(parameter): and use return to send back the output.
steps.demo_code:
def double_value(number):
 return number * 2

print(double_value(5))
print(double_value(12))
steps.instructions: Load the demo and run it. Call the function with double_value(50) and print the output.

steps.title: 2. Default Values for Parameters
steps.concept: You can give parameters a default fallback value. If the function is called without that input, it will use the default value automatically.
steps.demo_code:
def greet(name="Friend"):
 return f"Hello, {name}!"

print(greet("Alice"))
print(greet()) # Uses the default 'Friend'
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change the default name "Friend" to "Explorer" and run it.

steps.title: 3. Python Modules (Toolboxes)
steps.concept: Load built-in Python modules using import to access extra functions. For example: math.sqrt() for square roots or random.randint() for random numbers.
steps.demo_code:
import math
import random

print("Square root of 64 is:", math.sqrt(64))
print("Dice roll result:", random.randint(1, 6))
steps.instructions: Load the demo and run it. Click run multiple times to see different random dice rolls!

labs.title: Exercise 1: Fahrenheit to Celsius
labs.instructions: Write a function to_celsius(fahrenheit) that converts temperature using the formula: (fahrenheit - 32) * 5 / 9 and returns the Celsius equivalent.
labs.instructions_variant: Write a function inches_to_cm(inches) that converts length using the formula: inches * 2.54 and returns the centimeter equivalent.

labs.title: Exercise 2: Power Calculator
labs.instructions: Write a function power(base, exponent=2) that returns base raised to the power of exponent.
labs.instructions_variant: Write a function multiply(a, b=5) that returns a multiplied by b.

labs.title: Exercise 3: Roller Coaster Entry Check
labs.instructions: Write a function can_ride(height, age) that returns True if height >= 48 AND age >= 8. Otherwise, return False.
labs.instructions_variant: Write a function can_vote(is_registered, age) that returns True if is_registered is True AND age >= 18. Otherwise, return False.

modules.title: Module 5: Interactive Guessing Game
modules.description: Combine your knowledge to build an interactive number guessing game.

steps.title: 1. Project Brief: Guessing Game
steps.concept:
You are going to build a High-Low Guessing Game! It will pick a random secret number from 1 to 50, and give the player 6 attempts. In this step, review the blueprint instructions.

You will use: loops, conditional logic, user inputs, integer type conversion, and import random.
steps.demo_code:
# Ready to start building your game in the Lab tab?
print("Click on the Lab tab above to start coding!")
steps.instructions: Read the blueprint explanation on the left, then click on the Lab tab above to build the project.

labs.title: Exercise 1: Build the Guessing Game
labs.instructions: Write a full High-Low Guessing Game! The computer chooses a random number from 1 to 50. The player gets 6 attempts. Use input() to read guesses, convert them to integers, decrement attempts, and tell the player if they are too high, too low, or won! (Check the solution tab if you get stuck).
labs.initial_code:
# High-Low Guessing Game
import random

secret_number = random.randint(1, 50)
attempts_left = 6

print("I am thinking of a number between 1 and 50!")
# Start your loop here...
labs.example_code:
# Example structure of an interactive console loop:
import random
secret = random.randint(1, 10)

# Read input and convert to integer
user_input = input("Guess a number from 1 to 10: ")
guess = int(user_input)

if guess == secret:
 print("Correct!")
else:
 print(f"Wrong, it was {secret}")
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
import random
secret_number = random.randint(1, 50)
attempts_left = 6
print("I am thinking of a number between 1 and 50!")
while attempts_left > 0:
    guess = int(input("Take a guess: "))
    attempts_left = attempts_left - 1
    if guess == secret_number:
        print("You won!")
        break
    elif guess < secret_number:
        print("Too low!")
    else:
        print("Too high!")
if attempts_left == 0 and guess != secret_number:
    print(f"Game over. The number was {secret_number}.")
labs.instructions_variant: Write a custom number guessing game where the computer picks a random secret number from 1 to 20, and the player gets 4 attempts to guess it. Output "Too low!", "Too high!", or "You won!".
labs.initial_code_variant:
# Variant Guessing Game (1 to 20, 4 attempts)
import random

secret_number = random.randint(1, 20)
attempts_left = 4

print("Guess a number from 1 to 20!")
# Start your loop here...
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example structure of an interactive console loop:
import random
secret = random.randint(1, 5)
guess = int(input("Guess (1-5): "))
if guess == secret:
 print("Got it!")
else:
 print("Miss!")
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
import random
secret_number = random.randint(1, 20)
attempts_left = 4
print("Guess a number from 1 to 20!")
while attempts_left > 0:
    guess = int(input("Take a guess: "))
    attempts_left = attempts_left - 1
    if guess == secret_number:
        print("You won!")
        break
    elif guess < secret_number:
        print("Too low!")
    else:
        print("Too high!")
